info.topictitle: D.3.3 Magnetic forces on moving charges and current-carrying conductors

markdown:
## The Magnitude and Direction of the Force on a Charge Moving in a Magnetic Field


1. When a **charged particle** moves through a **magnetic field **, it experiences a force known as the **magnetic force**.
2. This force depends on the charge, velocity, magnetic field strength, and the angle between the velocity and the magnetic field.

### The Magnitude of the Magnetic Force


The **magnitude** of the magnetic force on a moving charge is given by the equation:

$$F = qvB \sin \theta$$

where:

1. $F$ is the magnetic force (in newtons, N)
2. $q$ is the charge of the particle (in coulombs, C)
3. $v$ is the speed of the particle (in meters per second, m/s)
4. $B$ is the magnetic field strength (in teslas, T)
5. $\theta$ is the angle between the velocity vector and the magnetic field vector

<callout type="note">* The force is **maximized** when the charge moves **perpendicular** to the magnetic field ($\theta = 90^\circ$), as $\sin 90^\circ = 1$.
* If the charge moves **parallel** to the field ($\theta = 0^\circ$ or $180^\circ$), the force is **zero** because $\sin 0^\circ = 0$.</callout>

### The Direction of the Magnetic Force


The direction of the magnetic force is determined by the **right-hand rule**:

1. Point your fingers in the direction of the velocity ($\vec{v}$).
2. Curl your fingers toward the direction of the magnetic field ($\vec{B}$).
3. Your thumb points in the direction of the force ($\vec{F}$) for a **positive charge **. 
4. For a **negative charge **, the force is in the **opposite** direction.

Otherwise, you can also use **Fleming's left-hand rule**:

1. Stretch out your **thumb**, **forefinger**, and **middle finger** so that they are **mutually perpendicular**.
2. Point your **forefinger** in the direction of the **magnetic field** ($\vec{B}$).
3. Point your **middle finger** in the direction of the **current** ($\vec{I}$).
4. Your **thumb** then points in the direction of the **force** ($\vec{F}$) acting on the conductor.

<callout type="example">* A proton ($q = +1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$) moves at $2 \times 10^6 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ perpendicular to a magnetic field of $0.5 \text{ T}$. 
* The** force on the proton** is:

$$F = qvB \sin \theta = (1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C})(2 \times 10^{6} \text{ m/s})(0.5 \text{ T}) \sin 90^{\circ} = 1.6 \times 10^{-13} \text{ N}$$</callout>

![Fleming's left hand rule showing thumb for force, first finger for magnetic field, and second finger for current.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/1917f712f0917c22983b23cd72269823eaca6120-990x834.png)

## The Magnitude and Direction of the Force on a Current-Carrying Conductor in a Magnetic Field


1. A **current-carrying conductor** in a magnetic field also experiences a force. 
2. This force is the result of the magnetic forces acting on the moving charges within the conductor.

### The Magnitude of the Magnetic Force


The **magnitude** of the force on a straight conductor of length $L$ carrying a current $I$ in a magnetic field $B$ is given by:

$$F = BIL \sin \theta$$

where:

1. $F$ is the magnetic force (in newtons, N)
2. $B$ is the magnetic field strength (in teslas, T)
3. $I$ is the current (in amperes, A)
4. $L$ is the length of the conductor in the field (in meters, m)
5. $\theta$ is the angle between the current direction and the magnetic field

<callout type="note">* The force is **maximized** when the conductor is **perpendicular** to the magnetic field ($\theta = 90^\circ$).
* If the conductor is **parallel** to the field ($\theta = 0^\circ$ or $180^\circ$), the force is **zero**.</callout>

![Current carrying conductor at angle theta to a uniform magnetic field, showing F equals BIL sin theta.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/8842cf34c2e4839cfdf554d764915c6ed62e11c4-1302x886.png)

### The Direction of the Magnetic Force


The direction of the force on a current-carrying conductor is also determined by the **right-hand rule**:

1. Point your thumb in the direction of the current ($I$).
2. Point your fingers in the direction of the magnetic field ($\vec{B}$).
3. Your palm faces the direction of the force ($\vec{F}$).

<callout type="example">* A wire of length $0.5$ m carries a current of $4$ A perpendicular to a magnetic field of $0.2$ T. 
* The **force on the wire **is:

$$F = BIL \sin \theta = (0.2 \text{ T})(4 \text{ A})(0.5 \text{ m}) \sin 90^\circ = 0.4 \text{ N}$$</callout>

## The Force per Unit Length Between Parallel Wires


1. When two parallel wires carry currents, they exert forces on each other due to their magnetic fields. 
2. This phenomenon is the basis for the **definition of the ampere**, the unit of electric current.

### The Formula for Force per Unit Length


The **force per unit length** between two parallel wires separated by a distance $r$ and carrying currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ is given by:

$$\frac{F}{L} = \frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2\pi r}$$

where:

1. $F$ is the force between the wires (in newtons, N)
2. $L$ is the length of the wires (in meters, m)
3. $\mu_0$ is the **permeability of free space** ($\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T m A}^{-1}$)
4. $I_1$ and $I_2$ are the currents in the wires (in amperes, A)
5. $r$ is the separation between the wires (in meters, m)

### The Direction of the Force


1. If the currents are in the **same direction **, the wires **attract** each other.
2. If the currents are in **opposite directions **, the wires **repel** each other.

![Parallel current carrying wires attract when currents flow in the same direction and repel when currents flow in opposite directions.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/135ae21eb68a7e4d0c12f25ae3192c99b9a7a02f-1511x729.png)

<callout type="example">* Two parallel wires $0.1$ m apart carry currents of $3$ A and $5$ A in the same direction. 
* The **force per unit length** between the wires is:

$$\frac{F}{L} = \frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2\pi r} = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T m A}^{-1})(3 \text{ A})(5 \text{ A})}{2\pi (0.1 \text{ m})} = 3 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N m}^{-1}$$</callout>

## Why Do Moving Charges Experience a Force?


1. The magnetic force arises because **moving charges** create a magnetic field, and this field interacts with external magnetic fields. 
2. The force is always **perpendicular** to both the velocity of the charge and the magnetic field, causing the charge to move in a **circular** or **helical** path.

<callout type="tip">* The magnetic force does **no work** on the charge because it is always **perpendicular** to the velocity. 
* This means the speed of the charge remains **constant**, but its direction changes.</callout>

## Linking Magnetic Force to Current-Carrying Conductors


1. The force on a current-carrying conductor can be understood by considering the** forces on the individual charges** moving within the conductor. 
2. The** total force on the conductor** is the sum of the forces on all the moving charges.

<callout type="example">* A wire of length $0.2$ m carries a current of $2$ A at an angle of $30^\circ$ to a magnetic field of $0.3$ T. 
* The **force on the wire** is:

$$F = BIL \sin \theta = (0.3 \text{ T})(2 \text{ A})(0.2 \text{ m}) \sin 30^\circ = 0.06 \text{ N}$$</callout>

<callout type="self_review">* An electron ($q = -1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C) moves at $1 \times 10^7 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ perpendicular to a magnetic field of $0.1$ T. What is the magnitude and direction of the force on the electron? 
* A wire of length $0.3$ m carries a current of $5$ A at an angle of $60^\circ$ to a magnetic field of $0.4$ T. What is the force on the wire? 
* Two parallel wires $0.05$ m apart carry currents of $4$ A and $6$ A in opposite directions. What is the force per unit length between the wires? Are they attracting or repelling each other?</callout>
